summary: This lesson introduces 'se' as the reflexive pronoun for third person (himself/herself/themselves/itself), completing the reflexive pronoun set. It practices conjugating quedar/quedarse across all persons and reviews darse cuenta with he/she/they forms now possible with se.

teachings.summary: To express 'himself', 'herself', 'themselves', 'itself', or 'oneself', lo/la/los/las all become 'se'. This is the third-person reflexive. Me/te/nos already include their reflexive meanings (myself/yourself/ourselves). Se is also the dictionary form for reflexive verbs: quedarse, darse cuenta.
teachings.rule: lo/la/los/las -> se for himself/herself/itself/themselves/oneself. Se does not change for singular/plural. In dictionary, reflexive verbs appear as -se form (quedarse, darse).
teachings.notes: Se is easy to remember because it's the beginning of 'self'. In the dictionary, quedarse (to stay) is separate from quedar (to remain). The se/-me/-te/-nos attaches to infinitives as one word: quedarse, quedarme.

teachings.summary: Practiced full conjugation of quedarse (to stay) with all persons including the new third-person se. Also practiced with emphasis pronouns (yo me quedo, tú te quedas, etc.).
teachings.rule: When a reflexive verb has a different meaning from its non-reflexive form, it gets a separate dictionary entry. Quedar = to remain. Quedarse = to stay.
teachings.notes: Nosotras nos quedamos for all-female group. Me queda bien = it fits me well / it suits me well (different meaning of quedar).
